one_line_takeaway: CatchBackdoor detects DNN backdoors by fuzzing a benign 'neural path' into a critical trojan path and thresholding a Label Change Rate, giving trigger-size-independent, data-free detection that roughly doubles LCR on stealthy attacks but trails K-Arm on clean-label backdoors.
key_finding: CatchBackdoor detects all trojaned models tested and achieves the highest LCR in most cells of the main comparison (Table 2), with roughly 2x advantage on stealthy attacks (blending and defense-adaptive). It is markedly more robust to trigger size: on LeNet-5/MNIST and AlexNet/CIFAR-10 it still detects (LCR >50%) triggers up to 22x22 where NC and K-Arm fail (they return non-detection beyond 10x10-13x13). It also detects backdoors from random noise alone (no training data) at ~73% average LCR, and holds LCR above 85% under both adaptive attacks (S-AA and I-AA).
core_contribution: CatchBackdoor is a model-inspection backdoor (trojan) detection method for image DNNs, built on the paper's own concept of a 'neural path' — a sequence of top-k critical neurons (ranked by contribution, defined as the gradient of the loss w.r.t. a neuron's activation) plus their data-flow connections. The paper's empirical insight is that trojaned behaviors across diverse attack families converge to a single 'critical trojan path.' The method: (1) builds a benign path from a benign seed (or random noise) fed to the DNN; (2) uses differential fuzzing to maximize neuron contributions, converging the benign path to the critical trojan path; (3) reverse-engineers a trigger by taking the partial derivative of the critical trojan path w.r.t. the input and adding it (transparency mu=0.5) to seeds; (4) flags a model as trojaned via a Label Change Rate (LCR) exceeding threshold lambda=0.5. Distinguishing claims vs. prior reverse-engineering methods (NC, TABOR, ABS, cited as related work): CatchBackdoor is independent of trigger size and can operate without benign training data.
threat_model: White-box defender inspecting a pre-trained model (e.g., downloaded from Model Zoo / Hugging Face) with no prior knowledge of whether/what backdoor exists. Defender needs only a small clean validation set (at least one input per label) OR can reverse triggers purely from random noise. Attacker is assumed to have training-data access (edit/add training data), knowledge of architecture and training algorithm, and injects exactly one trigger mapping to one trojaned label; attacker may trojan from scratch, fine-tune, or retrain with selected neurons/weights. Includes an adaptive-attack setting where the attacker knows the detector in advance (S-AA standardized and I-AA imitation adaptive attacks).
limitations: The paper itself reports CatchBackdoor is inferior to K-Arm on clean-label attacks (BullseyePolytope and Sleeper Agent), notably on LeNet-4 and AlexNet, attributing this to the critical neural path being weakly correlated with the clean trojaned label. LCR degrades on larger/deeper models — e.g. ~76% average on VGG19/a-ImageNet — due to neuron redundancy diluting path activation. DFST cannot handle gray-scale images so it is not evaluated on MNIST. The threat model is restricted to a single trigger with a single target label, requires white-box access, and its convergence 'proposition' is argued/assumed rather than proven; convergence is declared heuristically when path composition stabilizes (IoU plateaus around 0.6 after ~35 iterations). Much detail (pseudo-code, ViT/DeiT and low-trojan-rate extensions, adaptive-attack construction) is deferred to supplementary materials not in the main text.

quantitative_results: LCR detection threshold lambda=0.5; AUC >0.9 when lambda is between 0.4 and 0.6
Consistency rate after replacing top-1 neural path (Table 1): LeNet-5/MNIST 96.6% (class 1), 94.2% (class 5), 86.2% (class 9); AlexNet/CIFAR-10 87.4%/90.4%/84.4%
Critical trojan path IoU stabilizes around 0.6 after the 35th fuzzing iteration, independent of benign/random-noise/other-dataset seed
Table 2 example LCRs (CatchBackdoor): Poison frogs 94.0% on LeNet-1/MNIST; BadNets 93.6% on AlexNet/CIFAR-10; DFST 86.0% on LeNet-1/MNIST vs UNICORN 81.2%

Trigger-size robustness (Table 3, BadNets, 5% poison): CatchBackdoor detects up to 22x22 (LeNet-5/MNIST 56.2%, AlexNet/CIFAR-10 70.2%) where NC returns non-detection beyond 7x7 and K-Arm beyond 10x10-13x13
Detection without training data (random noise, Table 4): ~73% average LCR; e.g. BadNets 90.2% and ABE 71.4% on LeNet-5/MNIST
Adaptive attacks (Table 5): LCR over 85% for both S-AA and I-AA across all six models (e.g. LeNet-4 S-AA LCR 88.3%)
LCR degrades on deep models: ~76% average on VGG19/a-ImageNet
All trojaned models evaluated have ASR >90%; 50 benign and 50 trojaned models used for LCR distribution analysis
